The **Kingdom of England** was a [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") on the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") from 927, when all of the [Anglo-Saxon kingdoms](/wiki/Heptarchy "Heptarchy") were united under the rule of [Æthelstan](/wiki/%C3%86thelstan "Æthelstan"), until 1 May 1707, when it relinquished its sovereignty along with [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") to form the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"), which would later become the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). The Kingdom of England was among the most powerful states in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") during the [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and [early modern](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period") periods.

The political unification of England was a complex process that took place over many decades. Beginning in the year 886,[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeynes199823–24-5) [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") reoccupied [London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") from the Danish [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") and adopted the title [King of the Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/King_of_the_Anglo-Saxons "King of the Anglo-Saxons") in order to reflect his control over both [Wessex](/wiki/Wessex "Wessex") and western [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia"). This style would go on to be inherited by his son, [Edward the Elder](/wiki/Edward_the_Elder "Edward the Elder") (reigned 899–924), and grandson, Æthelstan, both of whom greatly expanded the authority of the [House of Wessex](/wiki/House_of_Wessex "House of Wessex") during their respective reigns. In 927, Æthelstan conquered the last remaining Viking kingdom, [York](/wiki/Scandinavian_York "Scandinavian York"), thereby making him the first Anglo-Saxon ruler of the whole of England and the founder of the Kingdom of the English. In 1016, the kingdom became part of the [North Sea Empire](/wiki/North_Sea_Empire "North Sea Empire") of [Cnut the Great](/wiki/Cnut_the_Great "Cnut the Great"), a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") between England, [Denmark](/wiki/History_of_Denmark#Middle_Ages "History of Denmark") and [Norway](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Norway_(872%E2%80%931397) "Kingdom of Norway (872–1397)"). The [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") in 1066 led to the transfer of the English capital city and chief royal residence from the Anglo-Saxon one at [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester "Winchester") to [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster "Westminster"), and the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") quickly established itself as England's largest and principal commercial centre.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Histories of the Kingdom of England from the Norman Conquest of 1066 conventionally distinguish periods named after successive ruling dynasties: [Norman/Angevin](/wiki/England_in_the_High_Middle_Ages "England in the High Middle Ages") 1066–1216, [Plantagenet](/wiki/England_in_the_late_Middle_Ages "England in the late Middle Ages") 1216–1485, [Tudor](/wiki/Tudor_period "Tudor period") 1485–1603 and [Stuart](/wiki/Stuart_period "Stuart period") 1603–1707 (interrupted by the [Interregnum](/wiki/Interregnum_(England) "Interregnum (England)") of 1649–1660).
All English monarchs after 1066 ultimately descend from the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans"), and the distinction of the Plantagenets is conventional—beginning with [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England") (reigned 1154–1189) as from that time, the [Angevin kings](/wiki/Angevin_kings_of_England "Angevin kings of England") became "more English in nature"; the houses of [Lancaster](/wiki/House_of_Lancaster "House of Lancaster") and [York](/wiki/House_of_York "House of York") are both Plantagenet cadet branches, the Tudor dynasty claimed descent from [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III") via [John Beaufort](/wiki/John_Beaufort,_1st_Earl_of_Somerset "John Beaufort, 1st Earl of Somerset") and [James VI and I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") of the [House of Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart") claimed descent from [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") via [Margaret Tudor](/wiki/Margaret_Tudor "Margaret Tudor").

The completion of the [conquest of Wales](/wiki/Conquest_of_Wales "Conquest of Wales") by [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I "Edward I") in 1284 put Wales under the control of the English crown. Edward III (reigned 1327–1377) transformed the Kingdom of England into one of the most formidable military powers in Europe; his reign also saw vital developments in legislation and government—in particular the evolution of the [English Parliament](/wiki/English_Parliament "English Parliament"). From the 1340s, [English claims to the French throne](/wiki/English_claims_to_the_French_throne "English claims to the French throne") were held in pretense, but after the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War") and the outbreak of the [Wars of the Roses](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Roses "Wars of the Roses") in 1455, the English were no longer in any position to pursue their French claims and lost all their land on the continent, except for [Calais](/wiki/Pale_of_Calais "Pale of Calais"). After the turmoils of the Wars of the Roses, the [Tudor dynasty](/wiki/Tudor_dynasty "Tudor dynasty") ruled during the [English Renaissance](/wiki/English_Renaissance "English Renaissance") and again extended English monarchical power beyond England proper, achieving the full union of England and the [Principality of Wales](/wiki/Principality_of_Wales "Principality of Wales") under the [Laws in Wales Acts 1535–1542](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535%E2%80%931542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535–1542"). [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") oversaw the [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation"), and his daughter [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") (reigned 1558–1603) the [Elizabethan Religious Settlement](/wiki/Elizabethan_Religious_Settlement "Elizabethan Religious Settlement"), meanwhile establishing England as a [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") and laying the foundations of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") via [colonization of the Americas](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas").

The accession of [James VI and I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") in 1603 resulted in the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns"), with the [Stuart dynasty](/wiki/Stuart_dynasty "Stuart dynasty") ruling the kingdoms of England, [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"). Under the Stuarts, England plunged into [civil war](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), which culminated in the [execution of Charles I](/wiki/Execution_of_Charles_I "Execution of Charles I") in 1649. The monarchy returned in 1660, but the Civil War had established the precedent that an English monarch cannot govern without the consent of Parliament. This concept became legally established as part of the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688.
From this time the kingdom of England, as well as its successor state the United Kingdom, functioned in effect as a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy").[[a]](#cite_note-7) On 1 May 1707, under the terms of the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), the parliaments, and therefore Kingdoms, of both England and Scotland were mutually abolished. Their assets and estates united 'for ever, into the Kingdom by the name of Great Britain', forming the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain").[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

Main article: [Name of England](/wiki/Name_of_England "Name of England")

The [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") referred to themselves as the *Engle* or the *Angelcynn*, originally names of the [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"). They called their land *Engla land*, meaning "land of the English",
by [Æthelweard](/wiki/%C3%86thelweard_(historian) "Æthelweard (historian)") Latinized *Anglia*, from an original *[Anglia vetus](/wiki/Angeln "Angeln")*, the purported homeland of the Angles (called *Angulus* by [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede")).[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2004139–140-10) The name *Engla land* became *England* by [haplology](/wiki/Haplology "Haplology") during the [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") period (*Engle-land*, *Engelond*).[[10]](#cite_note-11)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The [Latin](/wiki/Middle_Latin "Middle Latin") name was *Anglia* or *Anglorum terra*, the [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") and [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_language "Anglo-Norman language") one *Engleterre*.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

The standard title for monarchs from [Æthelstan](/wiki/%C3%86thelstan "Æthelstan") until [John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England") was *Rex Anglorum* ("King of the English"). [Cnut](/wiki/Cnut "Cnut"), a Dane, was the first to call himself "King of England". During the [Norman period](/wiki/Norman_England "Norman England") **Rex Anglorum** remained standard, with occasional use of **Rex Anglie** ("King of England"). From John's reign onwards all other titles were eschewed in favour of **Rex** or **Regina Anglie**. In 1604 [James I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"), who had inherited the English throne the previous year, adopted the title (now usually rendered in English rather than Latin) *King of Great Britain*.[[b]](#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Anglo-Saxon England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Anglo-Saxon England")]

Main article: [History of Anglo-Saxon England](/wiki/History_of_Anglo-Saxon_England "History of Anglo-Saxon England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Britain_peoples_circa_600.svg/250px-Britain_peoples_circa_600.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Britain_peoples_circa_600.svg)

Southern Britain in AD 600 after the Anglo-Saxon settlement, showing England's division into multiple [petty kingdoms](/wiki/Petty_kingdom "Petty kingdom")

The Kingdom of England emerged from the gradual unification of the early medieval [Anglo-Saxon kingdoms](/wiki/Heptarchy#List_of_Anglo-Saxon_kingdoms "Heptarchy") known as the [Heptarchy](/wiki/Heptarchy "Heptarchy"): [East Anglia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_East_Anglia "Kingdom of East Anglia"), [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia"), [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria"), [Kent](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kent "Kingdom of Kent"), [Essex](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Essex "Kingdom of Essex"), [Sussex](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sussex "Kingdom of Sussex"), and [Wessex](/wiki/Wessex "Wessex"). The [Viking invasions](/wiki/Viking_invasions "Viking invasions") of the 9th century upset the balance of power between the English kingdoms, and native Anglo-Saxon life in general. The English lands were unified in the 10th century in a reconquest completed by 927.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeynes2014534–536-4)

During the Heptarchy, the most powerful king among the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms might become acknowledged as [Bretwalda](/wiki/Bretwalda "Bretwalda"), a [high king](/wiki/High_king "High king") over the other kings. The decline of Mercia allowed Wessex to become more powerful, absorbing the kingdoms of Kent and Sussex in 825. The [kings of Wessex](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Wessex "List of monarchs of Wessex") increasingly dominated the other kingdoms of England during the 9th century. In 827, Northumbria submitted to [Egbert of Wessex](/wiki/Egbert_of_Wessex "Egbert of Wessex") at [Dore](/wiki/Dore,_South_Yorkshire "Dore, South Yorkshire"), briefly making Egbert the first king to reign over a united England.[[13]](#cite_note-15)

In 886, [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") retook London, which he apparently regarded as a turning point in his reign. The *[Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle")* says that "all of the English people (*all Angelcyn*) not [subject to the Danes](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") submitted themselves to King Alfred."[[14]](#cite_note-aschron-16) Asser added that "Alfred, king of the Anglo-Saxons, restored the [city of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") splendidly ... and made it habitable once more."[[15]](#cite_note-17) Alfred's restoration entailed reoccupying and refurbishing the nearly deserted Roman walled city, building [quays](/wiki/Wharf "Wharf") along the [Thames](/wiki/Thames "Thames"), and laying a new city street plan.[[16]](#cite_note-18)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

On 12 July 927 the monarchs of Britain gathered at [Eamont Bridge](/wiki/Eamont_Bridge "Eamont Bridge"), now in Cumbria, to recognise Æthelstan as king of the English.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeynes2014534–536-4) The title "King of the English" or *Rex Anglorum* in Latin, was first used to describe Æthelstan in [one of his charters](/wiki/%C3%86thelstan_A "Æthelstan A") in 928. The standard title for monarchs from Æthelstan until [John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England") was "King of the English". During the following years Northumbria repeatedly changed hands between the English kings and the Norwegian invaders, but was definitively brought under English control by [Eadred](/wiki/Eadred "Eadred") in 954, completing the unification of England.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStenton1971361-19) In 1018, [Lothian](/wiki/Lothian "Lothian"), a portion of the northern half of Northumbria [Bernicia](/wiki/Bernicia "Bernicia") was ceded to the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland").[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERollason2003275-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Cnut_1014_1035.jpg/250px-Cnut_1014_1035.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cnut_1014_1035.jpg)

The dominions of [Cnut](/wiki/Cnut "Cnut") (1014–1035)

England has remained in political unity ever since. During the reign of [Æthelred the Unready](/wiki/%C3%86thelred_the_Unready "Æthelred the Unready") (978–1016), a new wave of Danish invasions was orchestrated by [Sweyn I of Denmark](/wiki/Sweyn_I_of_Denmark "Sweyn I of Denmark"), culminating after a quarter-century of warfare in the Danish conquest of England in 1013. But Sweyn died on 2 February 1014, and Æthelred was restored to the throne. In 1015, Sweyn's son [Cnut](/wiki/Cnut "Cnut") (commonly known as Canute) launched a new invasion. The ensuing war ended with an agreement in 1016 between Canute and Æthelred's successor, [Edmund Ironside](/wiki/Edmund_Ironside "Edmund Ironside"), to divide England between them, but Edmund's death on 30 November of that year left England united under Danish rule. This continued for 26 years until the death of [Harthacnut](/wiki/Harthacnut "Harthacnut") in June 1042. He was the son of Canute and [Emma of Normandy](/wiki/Emma_of_Normandy "Emma of Normandy") (the widow of Æthelred the Unready) and had no heirs of his own; he was succeeded by his half-brother, Æthelred's son, [Edward the Confessor](/wiki/Edward_the_Confessor "Edward the Confessor").[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiles1914114-22)

### Norman Conquest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Norman Conquest")]

Main article: [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest")

The peace lasted until the death of the childless Edward in January 1066. His brother-in-law was crowned [King Harold](/wiki/Harold_II_of_England "Harold II of England"), but his cousin [William the Bastard](/wiki/William_the_Bastard "William the Bastard"), Duke of Normandy, immediately claimed the throne for himself. William launched an invasion of England and landed in [Sussex](/wiki/Sussex "Sussex") on 28 September 1066. Harold and his army were in [York](/wiki/York "York") following their victory against the Norwegians at the [Battle of Stamford Bridge](/wiki/Battle_of_Stamford_Bridge "Battle of Stamford Bridge") (25 September 1066) when the news reached him. He decided to set out without delay and confront the Norman army in Sussex so marched southwards at once, despite the army not being properly rested following the battle with the Norwegians. The armies of Harold and William faced each other at the [Battle of Hastings](/wiki/Battle_of_Hastings "Battle of Hastings") (14 October 1066), in which the English army, or *[Fyrd](/wiki/Fyrd "Fyrd")*, was defeated, Harold and his two brothers were slain, and William emerged as victor. William was then able to conquer England with little further opposition. He was not, however, planning to absorb the Kingdom into the [Duchy of Normandy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Normandy "Duchy of Normandy"). As a mere duke, William owed allegiance to [Philip I of France](/wiki/Philip_I_of_France "Philip I of France"), whereas in the independent Kingdom of England he could rule without interference. He was crowned on 25 December 1066 in [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey"), London.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDouglas1964204–205-23)

### High Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: High Middle Ages")]

Main article: [England in the High Middle Ages](/wiki/England_in_the_High_Middle_Ages "England in the High Middle Ages")

Further information: [Angevin Empire](/wiki/Angevin_Empire "Angevin Empire"), [Norman invasion of Wales](/wiki/Norman_invasion_of_Wales "Norman invasion of Wales"), and [Conquest of Wales by Edward I](/wiki/Conquest_of_Wales_by_Edward_I "Conquest of Wales by Edward I")

In 1092, [William II](/wiki/William_II_of_England "William II of England") led an invasion of [Strathclyde](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Strathclyde "Kingdom of Strathclyde"), a [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") kingdom in what is now southwest Scotland and Cumbria. In doing so, he annexed what is now the county of [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria") to England. In 1124, [Henry I](/wiki/Henry_I_of_England "Henry I of England") ceded what is now southeast Scotland (called [Lothian](/wiki/Lothian "Lothian")) to the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland"), in return for the King of Scotland's loyalty. This final cession established what would become the traditional [borders](/wiki/England-Scotland_border "England-Scotland border") of England which have remained largely unchanged since then (except for occasional and temporary changes). This area of land had previously been a part of the Anglian [Kingdom of Northumbria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Northumbria "Kingdom of Northumbria"). Lothian contained what later became the Scottish capital, [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"). This arrangement was later finalized in 1237 by the [Treaty of York](/wiki/Treaty_of_York "Treaty of York").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamsay190882–83-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/King_John_signing_the_Great_Charter_%28Magna_Carta%29_by_English_School.png/250px-King_John_signing_the_Great_Charter_%28Magna_Carta%29_by_English_School.png)](/wiki/File:King_John_signing_the_Great_Charter_(Magna_Carta)_by_English_School.png)

King John signs *[Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta")* at [Runnymede](/wiki/Runnymede "Runnymede") in 1215, surrounded by his baronage. Illustration from *Cassell's History of England*, 1902.

The [Anglo-Norman invasion of Ireland](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_invasion_of_Ireland "Anglo-Norman invasion of Ireland") took place during the late 12th century, when [Anglo-Normans](/wiki/Anglo-Normans "Anglo-Normans") gradually conquered and acquired large swathes of land from the Irish, over which England then claimed sovereignty, all allegedly sanctioned by the Papal bull *[Laudabiliter](/wiki/Laudabiliter "Laudabiliter")*.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-25) At the time, [Gaelic Ireland](/wiki/Gaelic_Ireland "Gaelic Ireland") was made up of several kingdoms, with a [High King](/wiki/High_King_of_Ireland "High King of Ireland") claiming lordship over most of the other kings.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEByrne200440–47-26)

The [Duchy of Aquitaine](/wiki/Duchy_of_Aquitaine "Duchy of Aquitaine") came into [personal union](/wiki/Angevin_Empire "Angevin Empire") with England upon the accession of [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England"), who had married [Eleanor, Duchess of Aquitaine](/wiki/Eleanor,_Duchess_of_Aquitaine "Eleanor, Duchess of Aquitaine"). They remained united until [John Lackland](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England"), Henry II's son and fourth-generation descendant of William the Conqueror, lost the continental possessions to [Philip II of France](/wiki/Philip_II_of_France "Philip II of France") decisively after the [Battle of Bouvines](/wiki/Battle_of_Bouvines "Battle of Bouvines") in 1214. A few remnants of [Normandy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Normandy "Duchy of Normandy"), including the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands "Channel Islands"), remained in John's possession, together with most of the Duchy of Aquitaine.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner2009103-27)

#### Conquest of Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Conquest of Wales")]

Up until the Norman Conquest of England, Wales had remained for the most part independent of the [Anglo-Saxon kingdoms](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_kingdoms "Anglo-Saxon kingdoms"), although some Welsh kings did sometimes acknowledge the [Bretwalda](/wiki/Bretwalda "Bretwalda"). Soon after the [Norman Conquest of England](/wiki/Norman_Conquest_of_England "Norman Conquest of England"), however, some Norman lords began to attack Wales. They conquered and ruled parts of it, acknowledging the overlordship of the Norman kings of England but with considerable local independence. Over many years these "[Marcher Lords](/wiki/Marcher_Lords "Marcher Lords")" conquered more and more of Wales, against considerable resistance led by various Welsh princes, who also often acknowledged the overlordship of the Norman kings of England.[[26]](#cite_note-28)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") defeated [Llywelyn ap Gruffudd](/wiki/Llywelyn_ap_Gruffudd "Llywelyn ap Gruffudd") and his brother, [Dafydd ap Gruffudd](/wiki/Dafydd_ap_Gruffudd "Dafydd ap Gruffudd"), and so effectively conquered Wales, in 1282. He created the title [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales") for his heir, the future [Edward II](/wiki/Edward_II "Edward II"), in 1301. Edward I's conquest was brutal and the subsequent repression considerable, as the magnificent Welsh [castles](/wiki/Castle "Castle") such as [Conwy](/wiki/Conwy_Castle "Conwy Castle"), [Harlech](/wiki/Harlech_Castle "Harlech Castle"), and [Caernarfon](/wiki/Caernarfon_Castle "Caernarfon Castle") attest.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrestwich1997206-29)

### Late Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Late Middle Ages")]

Main article: [England in the Late Middle Ages](/wiki/England_in_the_Late_Middle_Ages "England in the Late Middle Ages")

Further information: [Wars of the Roses](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Roses "Wars of the Roses"), [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"), and [Great Slump (15th century)](/wiki/Great_Slump_(15th_century) "Great Slump (15th century)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Battle_of_Agincourt%2C_St._Alban%27s_Chronicle_by_Thomas_Walsingham.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Agincourt%2C_St._Alban%27s_Chronicle_by_Thomas_Walsingham.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Agincourt,_St._Alban%27s_Chronicle_by_Thomas_Walsingham.jpg)

Fifteenth-century miniature depicting the English victory over France at the [Battle of Agincourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Agincourt "Battle of Agincourt")

[Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England") was the first English king to have a [claim to the throne of France](/wiki/English_claims_to_the_French_throne "English claims to the French throne"). His pursuit of the claim resulted in the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War") (1337–1453), which pitted five kings of England of the [House of Plantagenet](/wiki/House_of_Plantagenet "House of Plantagenet") against five kings of France of the [Capetian House of Valois](/wiki/Capetian_House_of_Valois "Capetian House of Valois"). Extensive naval raiding was carried out by all sides during the war, often involving [privateers](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer") such as John Hawley of Dartmouth or the Castilian [Pero Niño](/wiki/Pero_Ni%C3%B1o "Pero Niño"). Though the English won numerous victories, they were unable to overcome the numerical superiority of the French and their strategic use of gunpowder weapons. England was defeated at the [Battle of Formigny](/wiki/Battle_of_Formigny "Battle of Formigny") in 1450 and finally at the [Battle of Castillon](/wiki/Battle_of_Castillon "Battle of Castillon") in 1453, retaining only a single town in France, [Calais](/wiki/Calais "Calais").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeward1978260-30)

During the Hundred Years' War an [English identity](/wiki/English_identity "English identity") began to develop in place of the previous division between the Norman lords and their [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") subjects. This was a consequence of sustained hostility to the increasingly nationalist French, whose kings and other leaders (notably the charismatic [Joan of Arc](/wiki/Joan_of_Arc "Joan of Arc")) used a developing sense of French identity to help draw people to their cause.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMock2011[httpsarchiveorgdetailssymbolsofdefeati0000mockpage145_145]-31)

The kingdom had little time to recover before entering the [Wars of the Roses](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Roses "Wars of the Roses") (1455–1487), a series of civil wars over possession of the throne between the [House of Lancaster](/wiki/House_of_Lancaster "House of Lancaster") (whose heraldic symbol was the red rose) and the [House of York](/wiki/House_of_York "House of York") (whose symbol was the white rose), each led by different branches of the descendants of Edward III. The end of these wars found the throne held by the descendant of an initially illegitimate member of the House of Lancaster, married to the eldest daughter of the House of York: [Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England") and [Elizabeth of York](/wiki/Elizabeth_of_York "Elizabeth of York").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams197325-32)

### Tudor period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Tudor period")]

Main articles: [Tudor period](/wiki/Tudor_period "Tudor period"), [Elizabethan era](/wiki/Elizabethan_era "Elizabethan era"), [Stuart period](/wiki/Stuart_period "Stuart period"), and [English Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation")

See also: [Tudor conquest of Ireland](/wiki/Tudor_conquest_of_Ireland "Tudor conquest of Ireland")

Wales retained a separate legal and administrative system, which had been established by [Edward I](/wiki/Edward_I "Edward I") in the late 13th century. The country was divided between the [Marcher Lords](/wiki/Marcher_Lords "Marcher Lords"), who gave feudal allegiance to the crown, and the [Principality of Wales](/wiki/Principality_of_Wales "Principality of Wales"). Under the Tudor monarchy, [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") replaced the laws of Wales with those of England (under the [Laws in Wales Acts 1535–1542](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535%E2%80%931542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535–1542")). Wales was incorporated into England, and henceforth was represented in [the Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England").[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliams1993124-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Elizabeth_I_%28Armada_Portrait%29.jpg/250px-Elizabeth_I_%28Armada_Portrait%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elizabeth_I_(Armada_Portrait).jpg)

Portrait of Elizabeth I made to commemorate the defeat of the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada") (1588), depicted in the background. Elizabeth's international power is symbolised by the hand resting on the globe.

During the 1530s, Henry overthrew the power of the Catholic Church within the kingdom, [replacing](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") the pope as head of his own English Church and seizing the Catholic Church's lands, thereby facilitating the creation of a [variation](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") of Catholicism that became more Protestant over time. This aligned England with Scotland, which also gradually adopted a Protestant religion, whereas the most important continental powers, France and [Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), remained Catholic.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEElton1977185-34)

The "[Tudor conquest](/wiki/Tudor_conquest_of_Ireland "Tudor conquest of Ireland")" (or *reconquest*) of Ireland' took place under the Tudor dynasty. Following a [failed rebellion](/wiki/Thomas_FitzGerald,_10th_Earl_of_Kildare#Kildare_rebellion_(1534–1535) "Thomas FitzGerald, 10th Earl of Kildare") against the crown by [Silken Thomas](/wiki/Silken_Thomas "Silken Thomas"), the [Earl of Kildare](/wiki/Earl_of_Kildare "Earl of Kildare"), in the 1530s, Henry VIII was declared [King of Ireland](/wiki/King_of_Ireland "King of Ireland") in [1542](/wiki/Crown_of_Ireland_Act_1542 "Crown of Ireland Act 1542") by statute of the [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland"), with the aim of restoring such central authority as had been lost throughout the country during the previous two centuries.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrattan1822114-35)

[Calais](/wiki/Pale_of_Calais "Pale of Calais"), the last remaining continental possession of the Kingdom, was lost in 1558, during the reign of [Philip](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") and [Mary I](/wiki/Mary_I_of_England "Mary I of England"). Their successor, [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"), consolidated the new and increasingly Protestant [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). She also began to build up the kingdom's [naval strength](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), on the foundations Henry VIII had laid down. By 1588, her new navy was strong enough to defeat the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada"), which had sought to invade England to halt English support for the [Dutch rebels](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") and to put a Catholic monarch on the throne in her place.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMattingly1962401-36)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartinParker19995-37)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVego1999148-38)

### Early modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Early modern history")]

Main articles: [Early modern Britain](/wiki/Early_modern_Britain "Early modern Britain") and [Stuart period](/wiki/Stuart_period "Stuart period")

The House of Tudor ended with the death of Elizabeth I on 24 March 1603. [James I](/wiki/James_I_of_England "James I of England") ascended the throne of England and brought it into personal union with the Kingdom of Scotland. Despite the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns"), the kingdoms remained separate and independent states: a state of affairs which lasted for more than a century.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

#### Civil War and Interregnum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Civil War and Interregnum")]

Main articles: [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), [English Interregnum](/wiki/English_Interregnum "English Interregnum"), [English Commonwealth](/wiki/English_Commonwealth "English Commonwealth"), and [English Protectorate](/wiki/English_Protectorate "English Protectorate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Cromwell_at_Dunbar_Andrew_Carrick_Gow.jpg/250px-Cromwell_at_Dunbar_Andrew_Carrick_Gow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cromwell_at_Dunbar_Andrew_Carrick_Gow.jpg)

*[Cromwell at Dunbar](/wiki/Cromwell_at_Dunbar "Cromwell at Dunbar")*. [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") united the whole of the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") by force and created the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England").

The Stuart kings overestimated the power of the English monarchy, and were cast down by Parliament in 1645 and 1688. In the first instance, [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England")'s introduction of new forms of taxation in defiance of Parliament led to the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") (1641–1645), in which the king was defeated, and to the abolition of the monarchy under [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") during the [Interregnum](/wiki/English_Interregnum "English Interregnum") of 1649–1660.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFirthRait191118–20-40)

After the [trial](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice_for_the_trial_of_Charles_I "High Court of Justice for the trial of Charles I") and [execution of Charles I](/wiki/Execution_of_Charles_I "Execution of Charles I") in January 1649, the [Rump Parliament](/wiki/Rump_Parliament "Rump Parliament") passed an [act declaring England to be a Commonwealth](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Act_declaring_England_to_be_a_Commonwealth "s:Act declaring England to be a Commonwealth") on 19 May 1649. The monarchy and the House of Lords were abolished, and so the House of Commons became a unitary legislative chamber with a new body, the [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_(England) "Council of State (England)") becoming the executive. However the Army remained the dominant institution in the new republic and the most prominent general was [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"). The Commonwealth fought [wars in Ireland](/wiki/Cromwellian_conquest_of_Ireland "Cromwellian conquest of Ireland") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Scotland in the Wars of the Three Kingdoms") which were subdued and placed under Commonwealth military occupation.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBullSeed1998100-41)

Meanwhile, relations with the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") had deteriorated. Despite initial English support during the Dutch War of Independence against the Spanish, tensions arose as the Dutch Republic emerged as England's principal commercial and naval rival. By the mid-17th century, it had become the foremost trading nation. In response the English, alarmed by their waning competitiveness, implemented stricter trading policies to curb Dutch dominance. The [First Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Dutch_War "First Anglo-Dutch War") which followed, however, failed to resolve the commercial issues.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer200739–40-42)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1989a207–213-43)

In April 1653 Cromwell and the other *[Grandees](/wiki/Grandee_(New_Model_Army) "Grandee (New Model Army)")* of the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army"), frustrated with the members of the [Rump Parliament](/wiki/Rump_Parliament "Rump Parliament") who would not pass legislation to dissolve the Rump and to allow a new more representative parliament to be elected, stopped the Rump's session and declared the Rump dissolved.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobisonFritze1996147-44)

After an experiment with a Nominated Assembly ([Barebone's Parliament](/wiki/Barebone%27s_Parliament "Barebone's Parliament")), the Grandees in the Army, through the Council of State imposed a new constitutional arrangement under a written constitution called the [Instrument of Government](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government "Instrument of Government"). Under the Instrument of Government executive power lay with a [Lord Protector](/wiki/Lord_Protector_(Protectorate) "Lord Protector (Protectorate)") (an office to be held for the life of the incumbent) and there were to be triennial Parliaments, with each sitting for at least five months. Article 23 of the Instrument of Government stated that Oliver Cromwell was to be the first Lord Protector. The [Instrument of Government](/wiki/Instrument_of_Government "Instrument of Government") was replaced by a second constitution (the [Humble Petition and Advice](/wiki/Humble_Petition_and_Advice "Humble Petition and Advice")) under which the Lord Protector could nominate his successor. Cromwell nominated his son [Richard](/wiki/Richard_Cromwell "Richard Cromwell") who became Lord Protector on the death of Oliver on 3 September 1658.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaunt1996204-45)

#### Restoration and Glorious Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Restoration and Glorious Revolution")]

Main articles: [Restoration (England)](/wiki/Restoration_(England) "Restoration (England)") and [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/The_arrival_of_King_Charles_II_of_England_in_Rotterdam%2C_may_24_1660_%28Lieve_Pietersz._Verschuier%2C_1665%29.jpg/250px-The_arrival_of_King_Charles_II_of_England_in_Rotterdam%2C_may_24_1660_%28Lieve_Pietersz._Verschuier%2C_1665%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_arrival_of_King_Charles_II_of_England_in_Rotterdam,_may_24_1660_(Lieve_Pietersz._Verschuier,_1665).jpg)

Charles sailed from his exile in the Netherlands to his restoration in England in May 1660. Painting by [Lieve Verschuier](/wiki/Lieve_Verschuier "Lieve Verschuier").

Richard proved to be ineffectual and was unable to maintain his rule. He resigned his title and retired into obscurity. The Rump Parliament was recalled and there was a second period where the executive power lay with the Council of state. But this restoration of Commonwealth rule, similar to that before the Protectorate, proved to be unstable, and the exiled claimant, [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), was [restored](/wiki/Restoration_(England) "Restoration (England)") to the throne in 1660.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris200547-46)

In 1665 the unresolved commercial issues with the Dutch led to the [Second Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Dutch_War "Second Anglo-Dutch War"), which culminated in the disastrous [Raid on the Medway](/wiki/Raid_on_the_Medway "Raid on the Medway") and forced the humiliated Charles in to an [unfavourable peace treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Breda_(1667) "Treaty of Breda (1667)"). The treaty eliminated a number of long-standing issues, and in the long-term made it possible for the two countries to unite against the expansionist policies pursued by [Louis XIV of France](/wiki/Louis_XIV_of_France "Louis XIV of France"). In the short-term however, Charles' desire to avenge this setback led to the [Third Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Dutch_War "Third Anglo-Dutch War") in 1672. Despite attaining French support this time, Dutch naval successes made Parliament unwilling to support Charles' war effort any further, and he was again forced to make peace.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer200759–60-47)

Following the Restoration of the monarchy in 1660, an attempt by [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") to reintroduce Roman Catholicism—a century after its suppression by the Tudors—led to the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") of 1688, in which he was exiled by the Dutch prince [William of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"). William and his wife [Mary](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England") were subsequently crowned by Parliament.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETroost2005219-48) William reoriented England's foreign policy to support the Dutch Republic in its wars against Louis XIV of France.[[c]](#cite_note-49)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsrael1989b37–38-50)

#### Union with Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Union with Scotland")]

In the Scottish case, the attractions were partly financial and partly to do with removing English trade sanctions put in place through the [Alien Act 1705](/wiki/Alien_Act_1705 "Alien Act 1705"). The English were more anxious about the royal succession. The death of [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") in 1702 had led to the accession of his sister-in-law [Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") to the thrones of England and Scotland, but her only surviving child had died in 1700, and the English [Act of Settlement 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701") had given the succession to the English crown to the Protestant [House of Hanover](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover"). Securing the same succession in Scotland became the primary object of English strategic thinking towards Scotland. By 1704, the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns") was in crisis, with the Scottish [Act of Security](/wiki/Act_of_Security_1704 "Act of Security 1704") allowing for the Scottish Parliament to choose a different monarch, which could in turn lead to an independent foreign policy during a major European war.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynch1992311-51)

A [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") was agreed on 22 July 1706, and following the [Acts of Union of 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707"), which created the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"), the independence of the kingdoms of England and Scotland came to an end on 1 May 1707. The Acts of Union created a [customs union](/wiki/Customs_union "Customs union") and [monetary union](/wiki/Monetary_union "Monetary union") and provided that any "laws and statutes" that were "contrary to or inconsistent with the terms" of the Acts would "cease and become void".[[49]](#cite_note-52)

The English and Scottish Parliaments were merged into the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"), located in [Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster"), London. At this point England ceased to exist as a separate political entity, and since then has had no national [government](/wiki/Government_of_England "Government of England"). The laws of England were unaffected, with the legal jurisdiction continuing to be that of [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales"), while Scotland continued to have its own laws and law courts. This continued after the [1801 union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") between the kingdoms of Great Britain and Ireland, forming the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). In 1922 the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") seceded from the United Kingdom, leading to the latter being [renamed](/wiki/Royal_and_Parliamentary_Titles_Act_1927 "Royal and Parliamentary Titles Act 1927") the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.[[50]](#cite_note-53)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Government")]

Main articles: [Government in medieval England](/wiki/Government_in_medieval_England "Government in medieval England") and [Elizabethan government](/wiki/Elizabethan_government "Elizabethan government")

Further information: [History of the English monarchy](/wiki/History_of_the_English_monarchy "History of the English monarchy")

### Territorial divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Territorial divisions")]

Further information: [Historic counties of England](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England"), [List of earldoms](/wiki/List_of_earldoms "List of earldoms"), [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book"), [County palatine](/wiki/County_palatine "County palatine"), [English county histories](/wiki/English_county_histories "English county histories"), and [English feudal barony](/wiki/English_feudal_barony "English feudal barony")

The [counties of England](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") were established for administration by the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans"), in most cases based on earlier [shires](/wiki/Shire "Shire") established by the [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons"). They ceased to be used for administration only with the creation of the [administrative counties](/wiki/Administrative_counties_of_England "Administrative counties of England") in 1889.[[51]](#cite_note-54)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungs_Jr.1979xii–xiii-55)

Unlike the partly self-governing [boroughs](/wiki/Ancient_borough "Ancient borough") that covered urban areas, the counties of medieval England existed primarily as a means of enforcing central government power, enabling monarchs to exercise control over local areas through their chosen representatives – originally [sheriffs](/wiki/High_sheriff "High sheriff") and later the [lord-lieutenants](/wiki/Lord-lieutenant "Lord-lieutenant") – and their subordinate [justices of the peace](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace "Justice of the peace").[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler20072-56)
Counties were used initially for the [administration of justice](/wiki/Administration_of_justice "Administration of justice"), collection of taxes and organisation of the military, and later for local government and electing parliamentary representation.[[54]](#cite_note-Hackwood-57)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]
Some outlying counties were from time to time accorded [palatine](/wiki/County_palatine "County palatine") status with some military and central government functions vested in a local noble or bishop. The last such, the [County Palatine of Durham](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_Durham "County Palatine of Durham"), did not lose this special status until the 19th century.[[55]](#cite_note-58)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Although all of England was divided into shires by the time of the Norman Conquest, some counties were formed considerably later, up to the 16th century. Because of their differing origins the counties [varied considerably in size](/wiki/List_of_counties_of_England_by_area_in_1831 "List of counties of England by area in 1831"). The county boundaries were fairly static between the 16th century [Laws in Wales acts](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535-1542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535-1542") and the [Local Government Act 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888").[[56]](#cite_note-vob_census-59) Each shire was responsible for gathering taxes for the central government; for local defence; and for justice, through [assize courts](/wiki/Assize_courts "Assize courts").[[57]](#cite_note-winchester-60)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

The power of the [feudal barons](/wiki/English_feudal_barony "English feudal barony") to control their landholding was considerably weakened in 1290 by the statute of *[Quia Emptores](/wiki/Quia_Emptores "Quia Emptores")*. Feudal baronies became perhaps obsolete (but not extinct) on the abolition of feudal tenure during the [Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), as confirmed by the [Tenures Abolition Act 1660](/wiki/Tenures_Abolition_Act_1660 "Tenures Abolition Act 1660") passed under the [Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration") which took away knight-service and other legal rights.
Tenure by [knight-service](/wiki/Knight-service "Knight-service") was abolished and discharged and the lands covered by such tenures, including once-feudal baronies, were henceforth held by [socage](/wiki/Socage "Socage") (*i.e.*, in exchange for monetary rents).
The English *Fitzwalter Case* in 1670 ruled that barony by tenure had been discontinued for many years and any claims to a [peerage](/wiki/Peerage "Peerage") on such basis, meaning a right to sit in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), were not to be revived, nor any right of succession based on them.[[58]](#cite_note-61)

The [Statute of Rhuddlan](/wiki/Statute_of_Rhuddlan "Statute of Rhuddlan") in 1284 followed the [conquest of Wales](/wiki/Conquest_of_Wales_by_Edward_I "Conquest of Wales by Edward I") by [Edward I of England](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England"). It assumed the lands held by the Princes of Gwynedd under the title "[Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales")" as legally part of the lands of England, and established shire counties on the English model over those areas. The [Marcher Lords](/wiki/Welsh_Marches "Welsh Marches") were progressively tied to the English kings by the grants of lands and lordships in England. The [Council of Wales and the Marches](/wiki/Council_of_Wales_and_the_Marches "Council of Wales and the Marches"), administered from [Ludlow Castle](/wiki/Ludlow_Castle "Ludlow Castle"), was initially established by [Edward IV of England](/wiki/Edward_IV_of_England "Edward IV of England") to govern the lands held under the Principality of Wales in 1472.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoldsworth1913502-62)

At the same time the Council of Wales was created in 1472, a [Council of the North](/wiki/Council_of_the_North "Council of the North") was set up for the [northern counties](/wiki/North_of_England "North of England") of England. After falling into disuse, it was re-established in 1537 and abolished in 1641. A very short-lived [Council of the West](/wiki/Council_of_the_West "Council of the West") also existed for the [West Country](/wiki/West_Country "West Country") between 1537 and 1540.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYouings196041–59-63)

### Taxation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Taxation")]

In the Anglo-Saxon period, the [geld](/wiki/Danegeld "Danegeld") or property tax was first levied in response to Danish invasions but later became a regular tax. The majority of the king's income derived from the [royal demesne](/wiki/Crown_Estate "Crown Estate") and the annual "[farm](/wiki/Farm_(revenue_leasing) "Farm (revenue leasing)")" from each shire (the fixed sum paid by sheriffs for the privilege of administering and profiting from royal lands). Kings also made income from judicial fines and regulation of trade.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuscroft201629–30-64) People owed the king service in the form of the *[trinoda necessitas](/wiki/Trinoda_necessitas "Trinoda necessitas")*—[fyrd](/wiki/Fyrd "Fyrd") service, [burh](/wiki/Burh "Burh") building, and bridge building.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJolliffe196152-65)

After the Conquest of 1066, the Normans continued collecting the geld regularly. They also introduced new sources of revenue based on concepts of [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism_in_England "Feudalism in England"). The king was entitled to collect a [feudal aid](/wiki/Feudal_aid "Feudal aid") when his eldest son was knighted, his eldest daughter married, or if the king needed to pay his own ransom. The heir to a [fief](/wiki/Fief "Fief") was also required to pay the king a [feudal relief](/wiki/Feudal_relief "Feudal relief") before he could take possession of his inheritance. The king was also entitled to his vassals military service, but vassals could pay [scutage](/wiki/Scutage "Scutage") instead.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuscroft201690-66)

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_England&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Military")]

Main articles: [English Army](/wiki/English_Army "English Army") and [History of the Royal Navy (before 1707)](/wiki/History_of_the_Royal_Navy_(before_1707) "History of the Royal Navy (before 1707)")

In the Anglo-Saxon period, England had no standing army. The king and magnates retained professional household troops (*see* [housecarl](/wiki/Housecarl "Housecarl")), and all free men were obligated to perform military service in the [fyrd](/wiki/Fyrd "Fyrd"). In addition, holders of [bookland](/wiki/Bookland_(law) "Bookland (law)") were obligated to provide a certain number of men based on the number of [hides](/wiki/Hide_(unit) "Hide (unit)") they owned.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuscroft201631-67)

After the Norman Conquest, the king's household troops remained central to any royal army. The Anglo-Saxon fyrd also remained in use. But the Normans also introduced a new feudal element to the English military. The king's [tenants-in-chief](/wiki/Tenants-in-chief "Tenants-in-chief") (his [feudal barons](/wiki/Feudal_baron "Feudal baron")) were obligated to provide mounted knights for service in the royal army or to garrison [royal castles](/wiki/Castles_in_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "Castles in Great Britain and Ireland"). The total number of knights owed was called the *servitium debitum* (Latin: "service owed"), and historian Richard Huscroft estimates this number was around 5,000. In reality, the *servitium debitum* was greater than any king would actually need in wartime. Its main purpose was for assessing how much scutage the king was owed. Scutage was used to pay for [mercenaries](/wiki/Mercenaries "Mercenaries"), which were an important part of any Norman army.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuscroft201697-68)
